items.md: Barks could look outside his window and see the San Jacinto Mountains, which form a backdrop in the story. The Estudillo mansion becomes Don Gaspar's rancho, and the Saboba Indian reservation and even the rock into which the ducks' car crashes are drawn from real life (Barks, interview by Barrier, October 5, 1974).
items.value: Barks could look outside his window and see the San Jacinto Mountains, which form a backdrop in the story. The Estudillo mansion becomes Don Gaspar's rancho, and the Saboba Indian reservation and even the rock into which the ducks' car crashes are drawn from real life (Barks, interview by Barrier, October 5, 1974).

items.md: In the book, Felipé marries Ramona, something the film only implies.

44. Barks could look outside his window and see the San Jacinto Mountains, which form a backdrop in the story. The Estudillo mansion becomes Don Gaspar's rancho, and the Saboba Indian reservation and even the rock into which the ducks' car crashes are drawn from real life (Barks, interview by Barrier, October 5, 1974).

45. In the book, Felipé marries Ramona, something the film only implies.

46. Dydia Delyser, *Ramona Memories: Tourism and the Shaping of Southern California* (Minneapolis: University of Minnesota Press, 2005), 127.
